channel.title: Paper Overview
channel.description: Deep-dive walkthroughs of individual scientific papers — background, methods, results, and discussion, one paper per episode.
channel.itunes:author: Nigel

item.title: Episode 1: Wastewater sequencing catches H5N1 in California
item.description: A medRxiv preprint from UC Berkeley, the California Department of Public Health, and collaborators sets up genomic wastewater surveillance for influenza A across three California treatment plants during the 2024-2025 flu season. They combine a custom probe-capture enrichment panel covering 11 IAV subtypes with an expectation-maximization algorithm that can deconvolute multiple co-circulating subtypes from one sample — something existing tools, built for SARS-CoV-2, cannot do. Wastewater tracked seasonal H1N1pdm09 and H3N2 in agreement with clinical sequencing, and consistently detected H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b at all three sites all season — while clinical H5N1 detections in people were sporadic and rare. Wastewater H5N1 sequences clustered mostly with dairy cow strains, with a smaller avian fraction; no PB2 E627K mammalian adaptation mutation was observed.
item.itunes:summary: UC Berkeley and CDPH-led medRxiv preprint: probe-capture enrichment + custom EM deconvolution recovers whole-genome influenza A from California wastewater across 11 subtypes simultaneously. Three WWTPs sampled weekly Nov 2024-Feb 2025. Seasonal H1N1pdm09 and H3N2 track clinical data; H5N1 clade 2.3.4.4b consistently detected in wastewater while clinical detections are sporadic. LA County mid-November H5N1 wastewater peak had no corresponding clinical signal. Phylogeny: wastewater H5N1 clusters mostly with dairy cow strains, smaller avian fraction; no PB2 E627K mammalian adaptation mutation.
